previous_statement: (Contrapositive for infinite-dimensional case). If π_φ(A)' is infinite-dimensional (i.e., dim_R(π_φ(A)'^{sa}) = ∞), then φ cannot be an extreme point of any set C defined by finitely many affine constraints. Equivalently, under finitely many constraints, every extreme point has a finite-dimensional commutant and the multiplicity bound applies. This is the useful form for infinite-dimensional algebras where infinite-dimensional commutants exist.
new_statement: (Contrapositive for infinite-dimensional case — amended). If π_φ(A)' is infinite-dimensional (dim_R(π_φ(A)'^{sa}) = ∞), then φ cannot be an extreme point of any set C defined by finitely many CONTINUOUS affine constraints. This is the contrapositive of Node 1.3: if dim_R(π_φ(A)'^{sa}) > m + 1 for any finite m, then φ is not extreme in C. Equivalently (by Nodes 1.4.1–1.4.4), under finitely many continuous affine constraints, every extreme point has a finite-dimensional commutant and the multiplicity bound Σ n_j² ≤ m + 1 applies. This is the useful form for infinite-dimensional algebras such as B(H) (Node 1.8) where infinite-dimensional commutants exist. This is a deduction by contrapositive from Node 1.3, depending on Nodes 1.3, 1.4.1–1.4.4.
